resources.string: Complete %s? The project will be moved to your completed projects.
Complete %d projects? They will be moved to your completed projects.
Delete %d projects? This cannot be undone.
Row %1$d highlighted about %2$d percent down the page
No overlapping instructions found on this page.
Pattern scan could not be converted to PDF.
Delete this yarn card? This cannot be undone.
Saved to My Yarn in Library
No sessions recorded yet. Start knitting to see your history here.
Delete this session from history? This cannot be undone.
Upgrade to Pro for full session history
Could not generate AI summary — showing basic stats instead
AI formatting unavailable, entry saved as typed.
Speech recognition not available on this device
Speech recognition needs a network connection
Current stitches must be greater than 0
Number of stitches to change must be greater than 0
Result is too large. Use smaller stitch counts.
Increasing more than current stitch count - some sections will have no knit stitches between increases
Always buy one extra skein — dye lots may vary
Sizes are approximate equivalents. Japanese sizes use a separate metric scale.
All data stays on your device. No analytics, no cloud sync, no accounts.
Connecting to Google Play — check your internet connection if this takes long
Could not read a yarn label from this photo. Try again.
Pro required to save yarn cards
e.g. "22 stitches and 30 rows per 10cm" or "width 30 cm, 22 stitches"
Couldn\'t understand the instruction — try rephrasing
AI is busy — try again in a moment
Battery limit reached — try again later
AI feature not available on this device
Camera permission is needed to scan yarn labels
Camera permission denied — enable it in Settings
Row counter widget for active project
Upgrade to Pro to use the widget
Evenly space increases or decreases across a row
Calculate your gauge and convert pattern instructions
Calculate stitches to cast on for a desired width
Needle sizes across metric, US, UK, and Japanese systems
Standard body measurements for garment sizing
Saved patterns from Ravelry and PDFs
Your yarn stash — brands, weights, and colors
Progress photos from all your projects
No saved patterns yet.\nFind patterns in Ravelry Search.
Stitch by stitch, row by row.
Great things start with a single cast on.
Insert needle knitwise, wrap yarn, pull through
Insert needle purlwise, wrap yarn, pull through
One or more loops on the needle
The public-facing side of the work
The private/back side of the work
Wrap yarn around needle to create a new stitch and decorative hole
Insert needle as if to knit
Insert needle as if to purl
Right-leaning decrease: knit two stitches as one
Purl two stitches together as one
Left-leaning decrease: slip 2 stitches knitwise, knit them together through back loops
Slip, knit, pass slipped stitch over
Slip 2, knit 1, pass slipped stitches over
Slip 1, knit 2 together, pass slipped stitch over
Slip 2 together knitwise, K1, pass slipped stitches over
Lift slipped stitch over the last worked stitch
Pick up bar between stitches and knit through back loop to increase
Left-leaning increase using the bar between stitches
Right-leaning increase using the bar between stitches
Knit into front and back of same stitch to increase
Purl into front and back of same stitch to increase
Move stitch from left to right needle without working it
Slip one stitch as if to knit
Slip one stitch as if to purl
Create initial stitches on the needle
Pick up stitches along an edge
Place a stitch marker on the needle
Move marker from left to right needle
One complete circuit in circular knitting
Short needles pointed at both ends, used for small circumference knitting
Short needle used to hold stitches when working cables
Two needle tips connected by a cable
Work into the back leg of the stitch
Slip 2 to CN and hold in front, K2, K2 from CN
Slip 2 to CN and hold in back, K2, K2 from CN
Slip 3 to CN and hold in front, K3, K3 from CN
Slip 3 to CN and hold in back, K3, K3 from CN
Hold yarn to the front (purl side) of work
Hold yarn to the back (knit side) of work
Wrap the next stitch and turn work for short row shaping
Knit on RS, purl on WS (or knit every round in circular)
Purl on RS, knit on WS
Knit every row (or knit/purl alternate rounds in circular)
Alternating K1, P1 with offset each row
Start of the row or round
The next row, round, or instruction
The row, round, or stitch before the current one
Edge stitch worked differently for a neat border
The primary yarn color in a project
Yarn thickness measurement: wraps around a ruler per inch
Number of stitches and rows per unit of measurement
Rip out knitting (rip-it, rip-it — like a frog)
Unknit stitch by stitch (knit spelled backwards)
Symbols follow standard conventions. Always check your pattern\'s symbol key.
Knit on RS, purl on WS
Purl on RS, knit on WS
Wrap yarn around needle to create a hole
Slip stitch as if to knit
Slip stitch as if to purl
Slip 1, K2tog, pass slipped stitch over

string-array.name: knitting_tips
string-array.item: Always swatch before starting a new project — even if you think you know your gauge.
Block your swatch the same way you plan to block your finished piece for an accurate gauge.
To avoid a jog when knitting stripes in the round, lift the stitch from the row below onto the left needle on the second row of the new color.
Keep your tension even for a professional finish on edges.
Place a locking stitch marker every 20 stitches when casting on large numbers.
Block your finished pieces — it makes a huge difference in the final look.
Count your stitches at the end of every row when working lace patterns.
Wind your yarn into a ball before starting — it prevents tangling.
Use a lifeline every few rows in lace knitting — thread waste yarn through all stitches.
Weave in ends as you go to avoid a pile-up at the end of your project.
Always buy one extra skein — dye lots may vary between batches.
Try knitting a gauge swatch in the round if your project is knit in the round — gauge can differ.
Use stitch markers to separate pattern repeats — they make counting much easier.
Read your entire pattern through before casting on to avoid surprises.
Keep a project notebook to track your modifications and notes for future reference.
When joining yarn, leave tails at least 15 cm long for secure weaving.
Practice new techniques on a small swatch before committing to your project.
Store your knitting in a project bag to keep it clean and prevent snags.
Take breaks to stretch your hands and prevent repetitive strain injuries.
Mark your place in the pattern with a sticky note or magnetic board.
Use bamboo needles for slippery yarns — they grip the stitches better.
When picking up stitches, use a needle one size smaller for a neater edge.
Twist your stitches on the first round after casting on to prevent the cast-on from twisting.
Measure your gauge after blocking, not before — many yarns change after washing.
Alternate between two skeins every few rows to avoid visible dye lot differences.

resources.string: Voice mode ended due to inactivity
Monthly AI command limit reached; basic commands still work
%1$d stitches total, %2$d per row across %3$d rows
Next reminder: %1$s, in %2$d rows. %3$d more after that
%1$d hours and %2$d minutes total on this project
No project summary available. Open the project first to generate one.

You can say: next, back, undo, reset, plus a number. Ask about progress, remaining rows, time, yarn, pattern, stitches, reminders, counters, or notes. Say note to save a note, or name a counter to change it.
Multiple counters are named %1$s. Rename one before using voice commands.
AI summary is not available right now
Stitch count set to %1$d per row
Voice minutes used up this month
Could not load patterns. Check your internet connection.
Could not load more patterns. Check your internet connection.
Could not load pattern details. Check your internet connection.

Gauge tells you how many stitches and rows fit in a measured area. Knit a small sample (swatch) and measure it to find your personal gauge. This ensures your finished piece matches the pattern\'s intended size.

If your stitch pattern repeats every X stitches (e.g. a cable every 8 stitches), enter that number here. The calculator rounds the body stitches, before edge stitches, to the nearest multiple so your pattern aligns.

Edge stitches are extra stitches outside the pattern repeat. Enter the total edge stitches to cast on; the calculator adds them after calculating the desired width, for example 2 for one stitch on each side.

Flat knitting: you work back and forth on straight or circular needles, turning at the end of each row. Increases/decreases happen at both edges.\n\nCircular knitting: you work in a continuous round. Increases/decreases are evenly spaced around the round.

Metric (mm) is the universal standard. US sizes use a numbered system (0–50). UK/Canadian sizes use a different numbered system. Japanese sizes have their own numbering. Always check the mm size to be sure.
When enabled, all calculators use inches instead of centimeters and yards instead of meters. Gauge is measured per 4 inches instead of per 10 cm.
